text: The surgeon begins the subcarinal lymph node dissection, carefully isolating and removing lymphatic tissue from the subcarinal region.
The surgeon exposes the subcarinal lymph node area while carefully preserving the vagal branches visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a dissection plane around the right main bronchus using a harmonic scalpel, ensuring precise separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon grasps the right main bronchus to create space for the harmonic device to operate, focusing on the bronchial and nodal branches.
The surgeon uses a harmonic device to dissect around the nodal branches near the right main bronchus, carefully navigating the surrounding branches.
The surgeon performs a clear dissection around the right main bronchus, using harmonic technology to separate tissue planes while preserving the vagal branches.
The surgeon performs esophageal and lymph node dissection, aiming to remove potential metastatic tissue from the neck to the diaphragm.

The surgeon immobilizes the esophagus to prepare for anastomosis, carefully avoiding damage to the nearby nerve. The nerve and esophagus are shown in close proximity, highlighting the need for precision during this step.

The final view shows the complete separation of the esophagus and removal of lymph nodes along the left and right recurrent laryngeal nerve pathways. The trachea and major airways remain intact, with the left hilum fully exposed.
The surgeon shows the intact left pleura, indicating it was not opened during the procedure. The diaphragmatic lymph nodes have been completely removed.
The surgeon has prepared the stomach in the abdominal cavity, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon widens the hiatus by cutting a portion of the right crus, creating more space in the surgical field.
The surgeon pulls up the stomach graft, revealing the suture site between the resected esophagus and the conduit.
The surgeon visually assesses the stomach graft, checking for resistance as it is pulled up. The graft is adjusted slightly, with the surgeon determining the limit of tension before stopping.
The surgeon prepares to cut the suture at the anastomosis site between the stomach graft and the esophagus.
The surgeon identifies the tumor location approximately 27 centimeters from the esophageal entrance and prepares to cut around this area to perform an anastomosis.
The surgeon uses scissors to grasp and cut tissue, focusing on the area around the suture site between the resected esophagus and the stomach graft conduit.
The surgeon checks the position of the Levin tube and instructs to pull the L-tube back, ensuring proper placement within the surgical field.
